data.title: Exxon Mobil Loses Ground on Q1 Earnings
data.description:
Exxon Mobil Corporation shares was in the minus column Friday. The oil giant announced first-quarter 2026 earnings of $4.2 billion, or $1.00 per share assuming dilution.

Earnings excluding identified items were $4.9 billion, or $1.16 per share. Earnings were $8.8 billion, or $2.09 per share, excluding identified items and unfavorable estimated timing effects that unwind in subsequent periods. Cash flow from operating activities was $8.7 billion, or $13.8 billion excluding margin postings, which primarily fluctuate with the fair value of underlying derivatives.

Shareholder distributions of $9.2 billion included $4.3 billion of dividends and $4.9 billion of share repurchases, consistent with the company's previously announced plans.

Said CEO Darren Woods, "This quarter demonstrated that ExxonMobil is a fundamentally stronger company than it was just a few years ago, built to perform through disruption and across market cycles. Events in the Middle East tested that strength with the safety of our people remaining our top priority. Those events also underscored the importance of reliable, affordable energy products and the value of the capabilities we have built to deliver them.”

"The underlying business delivered strong results, reflecting the benefits of the strategy we have consistently executed since 2018. We have grown advantaged volumes, optimized our operations, reduced structural costs, and strengthened our earnings power. The result is a more resilient, lower-cost business, grounded in advantaged assets, disciplined capital allocation, and execution excellence. That foundation gives us a durable platform to grow earnings, cash flow, and shareholder value through 2030 and beyond."

XOM shares dipped $1.44 to $153.07.

data.title: European Credit Spreads Do Not Reflect Present Risks — Market Talk
data.description: European credit spreads trade at relatively narrow levels and fail to reflect the risks from the Middle East conflict, RBC BlueBay's Mark Dowding says in a note. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index trades at 290bps, down from 363bps at the peak of the U.S.-Iran conflict, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. Euro credit spreads risk widening, Dowding says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Embattled U.S. manufacturers show their mettle, grow for the fourth month in a row despite Iran war
data.description:
By Jeffry Bartash

ISM shows strongest expansion since 2022 - but inflation is a big problem

A vehicle passes a massive data center under construction in California. A surge in demand for artificial intelligence (AI) is fueling a boom in data centers across the country.

American manufacturers grew in April for the fourth month in a row - the longest streak in four years - but an embryonic recovery faces fresh hurdles from rising oil prices and higher inflation tied to the Iran war.

The Institute for Supply Management's survey of manufacturers stayed at a four-year high of 52.7% in April for the second month in a row. Any number above 50% indicates business is expanding.

The rebound in manufacturing has come as a surprise. Businesses began the year in a cautious stance as they groped with the initial Trump tariffs and soft demand from customers.

Yet a Supreme Court ruling striking down the original tariffs and an accelerating boom in artificial intelligence has supplied fresh momentum to an industry that has struggle to grow for the past four years.

The simmering Iran conflict and high prices for oil, however, are likely to keep a lid on how fast manufacturers can expand. The Trump administration has also put into place replacement tariffs and companies have to figure out how to work around them.

The ISM surveys executives every month about how their businesses are doing. Manufacturers employ about 13 million people and still play a vital role in the U.S. economy.

Key details: Production rose in April at a slightly slower pace compared to March.

New orders, a sign of future sales, rose to a three-month high.

Employment contracted for the 31th consecutive month, suggesting manufacturers are unsure whether the rebound will continue.

A measure of prices jumped to a nearly four-year high in a sign of persistent inflation.

Big picture: Manufacturers, likely the U.S. economy itself, have shown surprising resilience in the face of a series of obstacles to growth.

manufacturers and the economy can grow any faster, economists say, there needs to be an

An end to the Iran war, subsiding oil prices and more clarity on the scope of the new Trump tariffs are necessary, however, to enable manufacturers and the broader U.S. economy to grow any faster.

Looking ahead: "Revenues are very strong, however, price increases are similar to a few years ago with the supply-chain crisis," a top executive at a chemicals makers told ISM.

Market reaction: The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA and S&P 500 SPX rose in Friday trading.

-Jeffry Bartash

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Venezuela's oil exports jump to highest since 2018, with more sales to US, India
data.description:
By Marianna Parraga and Mircely Guanipa

Venezuela's oil exports rose 14% to 1.23 million barrels per day in April, the highest in more than seven years, fueled by more sales to the United States, India and Europe, shipping data and documents from state company PDVSA showed on Friday.

The South American country has been draining oil inventories and recovering crude output in recent months following the U.S. capture of President Nicolas Maduro in January, which led to a flagship supply pact between the governments of U.S. President Donald Trump and Venezuela's interim President Delcy Rodriguez.

The agreement, coupled with U.S. licenses easing sanctions on the country, has allowed PDVSA's joint-venture partners and trading houses including Vitol and Trafigura to receive cargoes from the state firm for sales to refiners in the U.S., Europe and Asia.

In April, a total of 66 vessels departed from Venezuelan waters, compared with 61 ships that carried 1.08 million bpd of crude and refined products in March, according to the data, based on tanker movements.

The April average is the highest monthly volume since late 2018, before U.S. sanctions were imposed on Venezuela's energy industry.

data.title: NVent Electric Shares Reach All-Time High as FY Outlook Raised
data.description:
By Christopher Kuo

NVent Electric shares climbed to an all-time high after the company raised its full-year outlook for sales and earnings per share.

Shares of the electrical-solutions company reached their all-time high of $167.37 earlier Friday, and were recently up 12% to $159.34.

The company on Friday raised its full-year outlook for sales and earnings per share, topping Wall Street estimates. The company now expects 2026 sales growth of 26% to 28% and full-year adjusted earnings per share of $4.45 to $4.55. Analysts polled by FactSet are expecting full-year adjusted earnings per share of $4.20.

The company projected second-quarter sales growth of 28% to 30% and adjusted earnings per share of $1.12 to $1.15. Analysts are expecting adjusted earnings per share of $1.07.

The company posted a first-quarter profit of $142.4 million, or 87 cents a share, compared with a profit of $360.7 million, or $2.16 a share, a year earlier.

Adjusted per-share earnings came in at $1.09, beating Wall Street's estimate of 94 cents a share.

The company's revenue rose to $1.24 billion from $809.3 billion a year earlier, driven by data-center growth, it said. Analysts expected $1.1 billion.

Write to Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: Gildan's HanesBrands Integration Could Unlock Higher Valuation — Market Talk
data.description: Gildan Activewear's acquisition of HanesBrands and the integration of that business "should drive incremental sales growth and margin expansion," helped by Gildan's low-cost vertically integrated manufacturing capabilities, UBS says in a note. The analysts say that even with the upswing after the 1Q earnings release, the stock still has 77% upside to their price target. "Gildan is still in the early innings of the HanesBrands integration," the analysts say, noting that a series of beat-and-raise quarters over the next twelve months, along with an investor day planned for December 2026, should fuel strong EPS growth and a higher P/E multiple. Gildan is flat year-to-date, but up 35% over the past year. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Fermi's Ousted Co-Founder Vows to Keep Fighting — WSJ
data.description:
By Jennifer Hiller

Fermi co-founder Toby Neugebauer isn't going without a fight.

The power startup said Thursday that it had terminated Neugebauer for cause, a move that removed the entrepreneur from the company's board of directors.

In a statement late Thursday, Neugebauer described Fermi's move as "completely misguided" and said he would "continue to fight vigorously to maximize value for all shareholders."

"No one believes more in the future of what we built at Fermi than I," he added.

Neugebauer left the CEO role earlier this month but had remained on the board. He has called for the sale of the company, which he co-founded alongside former U.S. energy secretary Rick Perry.

Neugebauer has said that he, family members and other executives or management that have recently departed the company own around 40% of the company's shares.

Fermi aims to build an enormous data center and power generation campus in Amarillo, Texas, but so far lacks an anchor data-center tenant.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
